messages.content:
You are comparing two analyst responses to determine which better demonstrates knowledge of a specific finding from a longitudinal dataset. Question asked: Based on the SIEM data so far, are there any users exhibiting unusual access patterns? Finding to evaluate: data transfer volumes from a single account diverge from peer group norms in a sustained multi-week pattern Response A: **Yes – several accounts are standing out in the SIEM logs as exhibiting atypical access behavior.** | User | Why the activity looks unusual | Supporting SIEM excerpts | |------|------------------------------|--------------------------| | **sbrown** | – 40 files downloaded in a single day (≈ 169 MB), well above the typical 10‑30 file range seen for most users.
– Triggered multiple DLP alerts (file‑size threshold and a sensitive‑keyword match).
– USB storage device was blocked. | 2024‑09‑02 log shows 40 files / 169.4 MB and three DLP alerts (FILE_SIZE_THRESHOLD, SENSITIVE_KEYWORD_MATCH) plus a blocked USB storage event【ref_id: insider_threat_05_ep_001】 | | **dlee** | – Downloaded 40 files (≈ 174 MB) across three repositories in one session, the highest volume observed for a single user.
– No prior baseline of such volume for dlee. | 2024‑09‑09 log lists 40 files / 174.2 MB for dlee【ref_id: insider_threat_05_ep_008】 | | **rjohnson** | – Generated a DLP FILE_SIZE_THRESHOLD alert for a large .dwg file copied to a local USB device (medium severity).
– Accessed three repos and transferred 24 files / 129 MB, higher than his typical 20‑25 file range. | 2024‑09‑09 DLP alert for rjohnson and his access summary (24 files / 129.6 MB)【ref_id: insider_threat_05_ep_008】 | | **tchen** | – Received three separate DLP alerts in one day (encrypted archive, external transfer, cloud‑upload) indicating risky data movement.
– Downloaded 10 files but the alerts involve .dwg, .step and .pdf files being moved to external destinations. | 2024‑09‑09 DLP alerts for tchen (ENCRYPTED_ARCHIVE_DETECT, EXTERNAL_TRANSFER_POLICY, CLOUD_UPLOAD_POLICY)【ref_id: insider_threat_05_ep_008】 | | **agarcia** | – USB HID device was blocked, an event not seen for other users.
– Accessed two repos and downloaded 36 files (≈ 95 MB), a moderate volume but combined with the USB block it is anomalous. | 2024‑09‑09 USB event (USB_HID blocked) and access log for agarcia【ref_id: insider_threat_05_ep_008】 | | **hkim** | – Two USB printer devices were blocked (one allowed, one blocked) in the same day, suggesting unusual peripheral usage.
– Accessed three repos and downloaded 26 files (≈ 77 MB). | 2024‑09‑09 USB events for hkim (USB_PRINTER blocked/allowed)【ref_id: insider_threat_05_ep_008】 | **Interpretation** - **Volume spikes** (sbrown, dlee) and **large‑file transfers** (rjohnson) exceed the normal daily baseline (most users 10‑30 files, 70‑130 MB). - **Multiple DLP alerts** for the same user in a single day (tchen, sbrown) indicate repeated policy violations. - **Peripheral blocks** (agarcia’s HID, hkim’s printer) are rare and may point to attempts to bypass controls. These patterns collectively suggest that **sbrown, dlee, rjohnson, tchen, agarcia, and hkim** are the users with the most unusual access behavior in the current SIEM data set. Further investigation (e.g., reviewing the content of the transferred files, checking role permissions, and confirming legitimate business need) is recommended. Response B: User jmorris is showing expanded file access scope beyond their current project assignment. Download volumes are trending above the user's baseline and peer group average. DLP alerts for jmorris are elevated. Which response better demonstrates awareness of this finding? The response may use different terminology. Focus on whether the core insight is present, not exact wording. Respond with ONLY one word: A, B, or TIE /no_think
